props.covers: The four-facet contract every canvas shape satisfies — geometry, style, text slot, connection anchors — and what a future custom-shape API would need to implement to add a new one.

text.insert: in this engine is not a drawing primitive — it is a typed, agent-editable JSON record: a member of the closed

union, carrying axis-aligned geometry, a style surface, a text slot, and a set of connection anchors that other code (routing, rendering, validation) derives mechanically from those fields. There is no freeform vector path, no per-instance transform matrix, and no plugin-registered shape class — a shape is exactly what its object record says it is, and nothing else is allowed to read or write canvas state around it (every mutation flows through the single reducer documented in
). That closedness is deliberate: an agent (or a human) constructing or editing a shape only ever needs to produce valid JSON matching this schema, never to author code.
Figma/FigJam's own plugin API models the same problem differently, and the contrast is instructive. A FigJam shape is a

is drawn from a closed 31-value enum (SQUARE, ELLIPSE, ROUNDED_RECTANGLE, DIAMOND, TRIANGLE_UP/DOWN, PARALLELOGRAM_RIGHT/LEFT, the ENG_* engineering shapes, PREDEFINED_PROCESS, HEXAGON, STAR, SPEECH_BUBBLE, and so on) — not user-extensible; there is no plugin-API call that registers a 32nd
. Text lives on the node as a dedicated, non-resizable sublayer (
) whose box is fully derived from the parent shape's bounds — you cannot reposition or resize the text independently of the shape. Connectors attach via a
NONE, AUTO, TOP, BOTTOM, LEFT, RIGHT, CENTER
— resolved by Figma's own (undocumented) geometry against each node. Fills and strokes are arrays of

numeric array for dash/gap lengths, even though FigJam's own shape toolbar UI only exposes a color picker and a solid/dashed toggle over that richer model. And critically: Figma's documented extension path for a genuinely new shape-like object is not the Plugin API's shape enum at all — it's the separate Widgets API, a different persistent-object system built from ordinary layers. There is no supported way to add a 32nd

This engine takes the narrower, more literal version of that idea: the vocabulary is a TypeScript union validated by a hand-written schema, the document is plain JSON an agent can read and write directly, and "extending the shape vocabulary" means adding a value to that union plus the handful of functions described below — not authoring a plugin, a widget, or an SVG path in isolation. The rest of this document specs out exactly what those functions are, so a future custom-shape API has a precise contract to implement against.

— is built from the same four facets: geometry, a fill/stroke/border-style surface, a text slot, and a set of connection points. A new shape is defined by how it answers each of these four questions, not by inventing a fifth axis.
— a world-space, axis-aligned bounding box. There is no rotation field anywhere in the schema: not on
, not anywhere else in the object record. This is a deliberate, explicit contrast with Figma's
(a writable degrees value, pivoting around the node's top-left corner) and
header comment states this plainly: the anchor/outline algorithm ported from BlockSuite deliberately drops "no rotation (this repo's schema has no per-object rotation field, unlike BlockSuite's

) — anchors are computed directly against each object's axis-aligned bounds/outline." Every downstream computation — outline polygons, connection anchors, obstacle bounds for routing, hit-testing — assumes the bounding box is axis-aligned. A shape that needed rotation would require a new schema field plus new geometry math through every one of those consumers; nothing in the current model treats rotation as a future toggle on the existing fields.

This style surface landed first for sections specifically —
was originally a section-only field, and its type is still literally named
in schema.ts. But the mechanism it expresses — explicit fill/stroke/width/style overriding a semantic default — is general: any object type can set

object at all — no per-type plumbing is required to opt in. The intended fill/stroke vocabulary for explicit colors is FigJam's own pastel pair palette (

Compare to FigJam's own surface: the plugin API's

A second family renders an icon silhouette via

The remaining shapes carry dedicated, special-purpose text slots.

This mirrors Figma's own structural choice at one level and diverges at another: FigJam's

is likewise "owned" by its parent shape rather than an independent resizable node — you cannot move or resize the text box separately from the shape, exactly as our label/body spans have no independent geometry of their own. Where we diverge is granularity: Figma exposes exactly one text sublayer per shape node (with per-character-range styling inside it), while this engine gives each shape family its own placement rule and, for section/sticky/code-block, entirely separate named slots (title, author, code body) rather than one generic text box reused everywhere.
Every object exposes exactly four connection anchors —

(10 world px) outside the object's bounds, each projected inward onto the object's real outline — the point where a line from the object's center to that candidate actually crosses the shape's border. Where an object's outline is a simple analytic polygon this engine already models, the projection uses the
(a 4-point polygon at the bounds' top/right/bottom/left midpoints), and

— falls back to its plain axis-aligned bounding-box outline. This is documented in the module header as the same conservative fallback BlockSuite itself uses for diamond/ellipse/triangle shapes, not a gap unique to this engine.

fractional coordinate over the object's own (width, height), with
is present, routing uses it directly as the more precise anchor; when absent, routing falls back to
Which of these an interactive drag actually produces is decided by

(10 world px), contain the pointer are even considered; (2) among those, snap to the nearest of the 4 cardinal anchors if its
(8 view px, converted to world px by dividing by zoom) — this produces an

; (4) else, if the pointer is inside the shape's outline polygon, connect to
(id only — no explicit position, resolved to center at render time); (5) else the candidate doesn't win and the loop continues. If nothing wins at all, the result is
— a connector endpoint floating on empty canvas, unattached to any object.
Contrast with FigJam's magnet model: our
is structurally the closest analogue to Figma's
— an attached endpoint at an explicit local point), and our
is the direct match for Figma's
magnet. But this engine has no equivalent of Figma's
magnet values as endpoint-stored state — there is no "unattached but tracking a node" or "let the renderer pick a side" flag on the endpoint record itself. The closest analogue to
lives one layer up, in routing rather than the endpoint schema: when an endpoint has neither
, the routing layer picks a side automatically at render/route time, rather than that choice being persisted as an explicit

value on the endpoint. Figma's magnet is a closed 7-value enum resolved by an internal, largely undocumented geometry function; this engine's cascade is fully specified above, in this repository, in the open.

Distilling the four facets above into what actually has to exist for a new shape type to work end-to-end — this is the spec a future custom-shape API would need to implement against, whether that API is internal (a new value added to this repo's union) or externally pluggable:

props.title: Anchors resolve against the true outline where one exists, bbox otherwise

text.insert: Connection anchors and outline-snap points are computed against each object's real silhouette for the four shapes whose outline is a simple analytic polygon this engine already models — rect/rounded-rect, pill, diamond, arrow-shape — and against the plain axis-aligned bounding box for every other type (icons, sticky, section, code-block, predefined-process, and any future shape that doesn't add a case to

). This mirrors BlockSuite's own upstream fallback for diamond/ellipse/triangle (per the FIXME cited in the connection-overlay.ts header) — it's an intentional, documented conservative default, not a shortcut taken under time pressure. A new shape type is free to skip adding a true-outline case entirely and simply accept bbox-based anchors; doing so costs some anchor precision on irregular silhouettes (e.g. an anchor landing outside a chevron's notch) but nothing else — bbox-fallback shapes render and connect correctly today.

props.title: Shape vocabulary is closed and typed, not freeform SVG

text.insert: Extending the shape vocabulary means adding a value to

and touching the fixed set of call sites above — schema validation, default geometry/tone/style-shape factories, optionally an outline function, a render function, and this documentation — not authoring an arbitrary SVG path or a runtime-registered shape class. There is no plugin-style registration API, no user-supplied path string field, and no mechanism for a canvas document itself to define a shape the engine doesn't already know how to validate and render. This is the same tradeoff Figma's own Plugin API makes for its

enum (31 closed values, no plugin-time registration) — the difference is that Figma's genuine extension point for new shape-like objects is a wholly separate system (the Widgets API), whereas this engine has no equivalent second system: every shape lives in the one typed union, full stop.

The W5 implementation wave exercised this contract end-to-end at scale: 20 new shape types (ellipse through the icon-glyph family) were added touching exactly the call sites enumerated above — schema union, default geometry/tone/style-shape factories, outline functions for the true-outline subset, render functions, and this documentation set — with no new seams required.
For the full enumeration of all 37 shape types with their concrete geometry/tone/style-shape defaults and any per-type quirks, see
